leaders.name: Sam Altman
leaders.character_brief: High-altitude timelines, compact declarations, and a recurring willingness to put civilization-scale claims into a very small number of words.

presence.absence_quip: No verified LinkedIn account entered the record. The thought leadership remains elsewhere.

leaders.name: Dario Amodei
leaders.character_brief: Long-horizon forecasts delivered with the calm detail of someone who has already seen the footnotes from the future.

presence.absence_quip: No verified LinkedIn account was found. The essays appear to be handling the workload.

leaders.name: Sundar Pichai
leaders.character_brief: Measured platform optimism, large historical comparisons, and the steady confidence of a keynote that has passed legal review.

leaders.name: Satya Nadella
leaders.character_brief: Platform shifts, organizational transformation, and a talent for making the next interface sound like it has already joined the quarterly plan.

leaders.name: Clement Delangue
leaders.character_brief: Open-model advocacy with startup velocity: benchmarks become movements, releases become evidence, and exclamation marks arrive fully funded.

leaders.name: Jensen Huang
leaders.character_brief: Industrial-scale optimism, compressed product cycles, and enough factory metaphors to make every data center request a hard hat.

presence.absence_quip: No verified X account was found. The keynote stage appears to have sufficient bandwidth.

leaders.name: Elon Musk
leaders.character_brief: Short timelines, maximal stakes, and predictions delivered at the speed normally reserved for product renaming.

presence.absence_quip: No verified LinkedIn account entered the record. One professional network may already be enough.

cases.title: The Majority Report

evidence.exact_text:
Narrative violation: according to Stanford University research, local models can answer 71.3% of real-world chat and reasoning queries accurately, up from 23.2% in 2023. Obviously at a fraction of the cost and energy consumption of frontier APIs.

The obvious conclusion: you don't need a frontier model for most tasks. The future is multi-model: local, open-source, smaller and cheaper for the majority of workloads, frontier APIs when no other choices!
…more
evidence.excerpt: The obvious conclusion: you don't need a frontier model for most tasks. The future is multi-model: local, open-source, smaller and cheaper for the majority of workloads, frontier APIs when no other choices!

cases.charge: One benchmark result is being asked to carry the phrase 'most tasks' without a definition of the workload distribution.
cases.fair_defense: The post explicitly reserves frontier APIs for tasks where smaller local models are insufficient and argues for a mixed deployment strategy.
cases.rationale: The claim is concrete enough to examine rhetorically, but the court cannot infer universal task coverage from the cited benchmark alone.
